narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model estimated an 88.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in the earlier and later years of your program. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your position in the bottom decile for Year 3 grades, meaning you ranked in the lowest 10% of your school that year, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to peers who climbed higher in their class rankings. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10%, reinforcing concerns about foundational academic challenges that often persist. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—slightly below the typical 3.21 average—did work somewhat in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, it wasn't strong enough to offset the dominant impact of those school deciles. Your LSAT score of 29, well below the average of 36.7, and your plan to attend full-time (like most applicants at 92.7%) played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. In essence, the model saw your consistently low class rankings as the biggest red flags when stacked against students who typically pass the bar, who often rank in the middle or top deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven pattern from real bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift the model's view in your favor, as even small improvements in rankings have swayed predictions for similar applicants. You're clearly motivated—use that drive to build a stronger profile for the next round.
